speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome to Git Going with GitHub, episode 4: The Learning Room. I am Alex. Today we are going to make The Learning Room something you can explain, practice, and recover from when the interface surprises you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will be the voice of the learner who is willing to ask, what is this for, where am I, and how do I know I did it right?

speaker: ALEX
text: The big idea today: Your shared practice environment: challenges, PR workflow, bot feedback, peer review. We will name the concept, explain why it matters, practice the move, and point out the checks that tell you the work is real.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the episode should work even if someone has not read the chapter yet.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The transcript has to stand on its own. It can point toward practice, but it should teach the concept right here in the conversation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: What Is the Learning Room?: The Learning Room is your own private GitHub repository for the workshop. The next useful detail is concrete: When you accept the GitHub Classroom assignment in Block 0, GitHub copies the Community-Access/learning-room-template repository into the workshop classroom organization as the workshop organization/learning-room-your username.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Here is the learner-facing version. GitHub Classroom gives each participant their own repository for three reasons. Put another way, real open source projects are shared spaces, and you will absolutely contribute to one on Day 2 (accessibility-agents) and through the Bonus C challenge. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. Safety -- you can experiment, break things, and recover without affecting anyone else. Authenticity -- you practice real repository work: issues, branches, pull requests, checks, reviews, and merging. Pace -- you progress through the 9 Day 1 challenges as fast or as slow as you need; nobody is waiting on you and you are not blocking anybody else.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Step-by-Step: Accept Your Classroom Assignment and Open Your Repo: this is the very first hands-on step of Day 1. That matters in practice: By the end of this walkthrough you will have your own Learning Room repository on GitHub and your first challenge issue waiting for you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: 1. Open the assignment link has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: In the same browser where you are signed into GitHub, open the Day 1 assignment link the facilitator shared; The page that loads is hosted on classroom.github.com. Your screen reader announces a heading with the assignment name (for example, "Git Going with GitHub -- Day 1"); and If the page asks you to authorize GitHub Classroom to access your GitHub account, activate Authorize GitHub Classroom. This is a one-time step. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: GitHub Classroom may ask you to pick your name from a roster so the facilitators can match your GitHub username to the registration list.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is if a roster page appears, navigate the list with arrow keys or use Find-in-Page (Ctrl+F / Cmd+F) to search for your name. Step two is activate the link or button next to your name. Step three is if you do not see your name on the roster, activate the Skip to the next step link and tell the facilitator in chat. They will reconcile the roster after your repo is created. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat 3. Accept the assignment as decoration. The status page does not auto-announce when the repo is ready. That is not trivia. Use Browse mode and press K to step through links until you hear your repository link, or refresh the page until it appears. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, you now see a screen with a button that says Accept this assignment (or just Accept the assignment ). Activate it. Then, the page changes to a status screen that says something like "You are ready to go!" with a refresh option. GitHub Classroom is now copying the learning-room-template repository into the workshop classroom organization and granting you access to your private. After that, activate the Refresh link (or reload the page with F5) every 15 seconds or so until you see a link to your new repository. The link looks like https://github.com/the workshop organization/learning-room-your username. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: 4. Open your new repository has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. The repo name in the heading matches learning-room-your username. The About sidebar (or repo description) confirms this is a private workshop copy. You see folders like docs/,.github/, and files like README.md. These came from the template.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Activate the link to your repository. You land on the standard GitHub repo page for the workshop organization/learning-room-your username. Then: Verify three things on this page. Next: Bookmark this page. You will return here for every Day 1 challenge. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put 5. Find your first challenge issue into plain language. When your Learning Room repo is ready, Challenge 1 appears as a GitHub issue in your repo. The useful version is: The facilitators prepare this by running the Student Progression Bot after students accept the Classroom assignment.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: From your repository page, navigate to the Issues tab. Keyboard shortcut: press G then I; You should see at least one open issue with a title like "Challenge 1: Find Your Way Around" authored by aria-bot (or github-actions[bot]); and Open Challenge 1. Read the issue body -- it tells you what to do, where to find evidence, and how to submit completion. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid issue habit is to read the title, the body, and the timeline before acting. You are listening for the requested action, the missing evidence, and the person who needs a response.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. The PR validation bot, Aria, posts educational feedback whenever you open a pull request. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: To confirm Aria is wired up, open the Actions tab in your repo and look for a workflow named pr-validation-bot (or Aria PR Validation ).

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because chapter 4 is a system orientation chapter. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. There are none. Automation check: none. Why: this chapter explains how your repo is set up and prepares you for the issue-based challenges that start in Chapter 5.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Readiness Checkpoint. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Readiness Checkpoint has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, find docs/CHALLENGES.md in your Learning Room repository. Then, explain the flow: issue - branch - pull request - review - merge. After that, identify where Aria bot feedback appears on a PR (the Conversation tab). Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Here is the learner-facing version. Throughout Day 1 you work on two parallel learning tracks, both in your own account.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Track 1: GitHub Skills Modules (Optional Self-Paced Practice): scope: Your personal account, optional and self-paced Bot: Mona (GitHub's automated learning bot) guides each step Purpose: Hands-on practice of individual skills, complementary to the workshop.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. Introduction to GitHub - Create branch, open PR, merge. Communicate Using Markdown - Write headings, links, code, tables. Review Pull Requests - Comment, approve, suggest changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Track 2: Your Learning Room Repository (Required Workshop Track) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Blocks 1-4 (Day 1 morning/afternoon): Challenges 1-7 -- find your way, file an issue, branch, commit, open a PR, survive a merge conflict. Block 5 (Day 1 evening): Challenges 8-9 -- culture and merge day. Block 6 (Day 1 evening): Community tools (labels, milestones, notifications).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you translate that into plain choices?

speaker: ALEX
text: How the Two Tracks Compare has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the comparison to make a decision, not to recite a table. The main contrasts are: GitHub Skills (optional) means Your Learning Room (required). Create a branch in a Skills repo means Create a branch in your Learning Room. Open a PR means Open a PR.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Learning Cards: Two Tracks, One Account has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. GitHub Skills modules run in your personal account; press G I from any Skills repo to see the issue thread where Mona posts instructions. Your Learning Room repository lives at a different URL inside the workshop organization; bookmark it and use Alt+D (address bar) to confirm which repo you are in. Aria's bot comments on your PRs appear as PR conversation comments; press 3 on the PR page to jump between them. GitHub Skills repos have a distinct green banner at the top of the README that says "Start course"; your Learning Room repo has no such banner. Check the repository name in the top-left header to confirm which track you are working in (Skills repo vs. your Learning Room). Aria's avatar appears next to bot comments; your human reviewer's avatar appears next to peer review comments.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Your Learning Room Folder Structure is still useful because every Learning Room repository (yours and every other participant's) starts as an exact copy of learning-room-template and contains these files and folders.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. README.md -- Getting started guide.github/. STUDENT GUIDE.md -- How the bot works. IMPLEMENTATION GUIDE.md -- Full setup walkthrough. SETUP AND MAINTENANCE.md -- Maintenance reference. workflows/ -- 3 automation workflows.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Put Your Practice Branch into plain language. In your own Learning Room repository, you decide what branches to create. The useful version is: The Day 1 challenge sequence asks you to work on a single feature branch named after yourself. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. If your GitHub username is payown, your branch is learn/payown. If your username is BudgieMom, your branch is learn/budgiemom. If your username is Weijun-Zhang-1996, your branch is learn/weijun-zhang-1996.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Why you create a separate branch has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Protected main branch - The main branch in your Learning Room is protected; changes have to land via pull request even though you own the repo. This mirrors how mature open source projects work. Your workspace - Your learn/ branch is where you commit and push changes before opening a PR. Clean history - Keeping experiments off main until they are reviewed (by you, by Aria, or by a peer) keeps your project history easy to read. Realistic workflow - Contributors to real open source projects always create feature branches before opening a PR. You are practicing exactly that pattern.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to use your branch. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because the Day 1 challenges (4 onward) walk you through creating and using your branch on GitHub.com. That gives the learner a foothold: once you start working locally in Chapter 14, the same branch is what you check out.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is create the branch on GitHub.com (Challenge 4) or locally with git checkout -b learn/ from main. Step two is make your changes and commit them to the branch. Step three is push the branch to GitHub if you created it locally. Step four is open a pull request from learn/ - main (Challenge 6). If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git checkout learn/. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Your Practice Branch. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Your Practice Branch has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. To switch branches on GitHub.com, press W (in Focus Mode) to open the branch selector, type your branch name (learn/ ), and press Enter when it appears. In VS Code's terminal, type git checkout learn/ (lowercase). Press Ctrl+Shift+G in VS Code to open Source Control; the current branch name is announced at the top of the panel. The branch selector button shows the current branch name (e.g., "main") above the file table; click it and type "learn" to filter to your branch. After switching branches, the branch name updates in the selector button; verify it reads learn/, not "main". In VS Code, the current branch name appears in the bottom-left corner of the Status Bar in small text; zoom in or check Source Control panel header for a larger display.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Tool Cards: Switch to Your Practice Branch. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Tool Cards: Switch to Your Practice Branch has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: On the repository page, click the branch selector dropdown (shows "main"). Then: Type learn to filter, then select learn/. Next: Click the branch name in the bottom-left status bar. Last: Select your learn/ branch from the list. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Tool Cards: Switch to Your Practice Branch, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Click the branch name in the bottom-left status bar (or press Ctrl+Shift+P then type Git: Checkout to ); Select origin/learn/ from the branch list; and Click the Current Branch dropdown at the top. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on The Practice Files: What You Will Work On. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside The Practice Files: What You Will Work On: see also: Appendix C: Markdown Reference covers all Markdown syntax with accessible examples -- you will need it for editing these files. That matters in practice: The docs/ folder contains three practice files with intentional issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on docs/welcome.md - Introduction to Open Source Contribution. This file introduces newcomers to open source. This is the part to say slowly: It has three [TODO] sections where content is missing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on [TODO] 1 - "Who Can Contribute?" section. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: [TODO: Add a paragraph explaining that contributors come from all backgrounds, skill levels, and countries. The listener should be able to check this: Emphasize that using assistive technology is not a barrier to contribution - in fact, AT users bring a perspective that improves projects for everyone.].

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Do not treat [TODO] 2 - "Finding Something to Work On" section as decoration. [TODO: Add two or three sentences about how to read an issue to decide if it is right for you. That is not trivia. What questions should you ask yourself? The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on [TODO] 3 - "After Your Contribution Is Merged" section. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, [TODO] 3 - "After Your Contribution Is Merged" section is still useful because [TODO: Add a sentence or two about what this means for someone's GitHub profile and open source portfolio.]. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: It also has a broken internal link that needs to be found and fixed.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Put docs/keyboard-shortcuts.md - Screen Reader Shortcut Reference into plain language. This is a comprehensive reference with tables for NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver shortcuts. The useful version is: It contains intentional errors in some shortcut references that students need to find and fix.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. NVDA (Windows) - Single-key navigation, mode switching, reading commands. JAWS (Windows) - Virtual cursor navigation, mode switching, reading commands. VoiceOver (macOS) - Rotor navigation, VO commands for GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does someone need before they touch the keyboard?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. This step-by-step guide walks through GitHub account setup, accessibility settings, screen reader configuration, and repository forking. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: It contains broken links that point to incorrect URLs and incomplete steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. Links to GitHub settings pages that may have changed. A [TODO] note at the bottom referencing items for facilitators. Steps that reference forking a "workshop repository" without providing the actual URL.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. This part earns its place because this file lists all 21 challenges organized by progression level. That gives the learner a foothold: each challenge lists the file(s) to edit, estimated time, skills practiced, and success criteria. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should the learner prove to themselves after each small task?

speaker: ALEX
text: Bonus Challenges: Five bonus challenges (A through E) are available for students who finish faster. The next useful detail is concrete: These include an accessibility audit, mentoring a peer, cross-repository contribution, creating a custom workflow, and documentation improvement.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Student A (working on Challenge 3: Complete Welcome Guide). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. Visibility: The PR immediately appears in the repo's Pull Requests tab.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Finds their assigned issue (Issues tab → filter Assignee:@me). Then: Opens docs/welcome.md and edits the three [TODO] sections. Next: Commits to a new branch: fix/studentA-issue12. Last: Opens a pull request with description. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Student A (working on Challenge 3: Complete Welcome Guide), what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Submits the PR. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Bot (.github/workflows/learning-room-pr-bot.yml). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Bot (.github/workflows/learning-room-pr-bot.yml): visibility: The bot comment appears in your PR. That matters in practice: You see it; the facilitators see it; nobody else does unless they have been added as a collaborator on your repo.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. Runs within 30 seconds. Issue reference (does PR link to issue with Closes 12?). File location (only docs/ directory files changed?). Markdown accessibility (headings, links, alt text, broken links).

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step 3: Peer Review (Facilitator-Arranged). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on Step 3: Peer Review (Facilitator-Arranged). The learning-room-template ships with a peer-pairing workflow (.github/workflows/student-grouping.yml) that was designed for a single shared repo. This is the part to say slowly: Under the GitHub Classroom model used in this workshop, peer review is arranged by the facilitators rather than auto-assigned, because each student's repo is private. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: The facilitators (Jeff and Michael) pair you with another participant; They add each of you as a collaborator on the other's Learning Room repo; Each of you receives a notification: "You have been added as a collaborator."; and You can now read the other person's PRs, leave comments, request changes, and approve. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Step 3: Peer Review (Facilitator-Arranged), what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is after the challenge is complete, the collaborator access remains until the workshop ends -- you can keep helping each other as you work through the rest of Day 1. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Visibility. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Visibility has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. You see PRs in the repos you have been added to (yours plus any peers you have been paired with). Notifications show review requests in your GitHub Notifications inbox. Other participants in the cohort cannot see your repo unless they are paired with you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Your assigned peer reviewer (when one is paired with you). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Your assigned peer reviewer (when one is paired with you) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. PR title: "Complete [TODO] sections in welcome.md". PR description: lists which sections were completed. Aria's feedback: checks that all [TODO] markers are removed, heading hierarchy is valid. The actual file changes (Files Changed tab): sees the diff showing old [TODO] markers replaced with new content. Inline comment on the "Who Can Contribute?" paragraph: "Great addition - I especially like the point about AT users bringing valuable perspective.". Overall comment: "The content reads well and all TODOs are resolved. One suggestion: the 'Finding Something to Work On' section could mention checking if an issue already has an assignee.".

speaker: ALEX
text: First, receives notification: "PR review requested". Then, navigates to the PR in your Learning Room repo (they have collaborator access). After that, leaves review comments. Finally, submits review: Approve (or Request Changes if a [TODO] marker was missed). If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Visibility, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. You (the PR author) get a notification: "Your PR has a new review". The reviewer's comments appear in your PR thread. Their name shows in the Reviewers sidebar of your PR.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on You (PR author). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: You (PR author) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Read Aria's feedback and any human review; Talks to the reviewer if something is unclear; Makes changes based on feedback; and Pushes new commits to the same branch. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave You (PR author), what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 1 checks: Re-addresses the feedback. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the teaching move inside Visibility?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Aria re-validates on each new commit and updates its comment. Your reviewer sees the updated activity in the PR. The PR timeline shows iteration happening.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. When the review is approved (or you decide to self-merge) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. You merge the PR (button becomes available). PR closes, shows "merged".

speaker: JAMIE
text: What makes this practice feel low-stakes but still real?

speaker: ALEX
text: Progression Bot Posts the Next Challenge has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. The Student Progression bot detects the merged PR and the closed challenge issue. Posts a celebration comment (challenge badge earned). Auto-creates the next challenge issue in your repo so you can keep moving. Updates the progress tracking file.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Visibility has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. You see the merged PR and the new challenge issue. Your peer reviewer (if you have one) is notified the PR was merged. Other participants only see this if they have been paired with you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: How PR Sharing Works. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: How PR Sharing Works has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. When a bot comment appears on your PR, press 3 on the Conversation tab to jump between comment headers; bot comments include the bot's username in the heading. To find your assigned reviewer, press D to reach the sidebar landmark, then H until you hear "Reviewers" -- your reviewer's username follows. When you receive a "review requested" notification, press G N from any GitHub page to go to Notifications and find the review request. Bot comments are visually indented and often have a colored banner (green for pass, yellow for warnings); zoom in on the banner text for the summary. The Reviewers section in the PR sidebar shows a small avatar and username; at high zoom avatars may overlap -- read the text username instead. Merge button turns green when all required checks pass and the reviewer approves; it appears at the bottom of the Conversation tab.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. Anchor this part on The Learning Automation System. When you open a PR in the Learning Room, you get three types of feedback.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Type 1: Automated Bot Feedback (30 seconds). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Type 1: Automated Bot Feedback (30 seconds) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Technical validation (links, headings, file locations). Accessibility checking (detailed). Educational messaging (WHY each thing matters). Links to learning resources. Never fails the PR; always educational.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where confidence starts to build. Type 2: Peer Reviewer Feedback (15-60 minutes) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Human judgment on content. Creative suggestions. Encouragement and mentorship. Understanding of context. Can approve, request changes, or comment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Type 3: Progress Tracking (on merge). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Type 3: Progress Tracking (on merge) is still useful because together: Instant technical feedback + human mentorship + visible progress.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Skill badges (Markdown Master, Accessibility Advocate). Level progression (Beginner → Intermediate → Advanced → Expert). Milestone celebrations (1st, 5th, 10th PR). Motivational comments.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Study Groups (Optional). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put Study Groups (Optional) into plain language. If your facilitators create study groups, you will be paired with 2-3 other participants and added as collaborators on each other's Learning Room repos.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Group Issue Thread - Private communication space for your group; Shared Review Responsibility - You review each other's work; Collaborative Challenges - Optional group exercises; and Peer Support - Tag each other with questions. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should someone choose between those options?

speaker: ALEX
text: Key Differences: Skills Module vs. Your Learning Room has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the comparison to make a decision, not to recite a table. The main contrasts are: GitHub Skills (Your Account) means Your Learning Room (Classroom). Your personal copy of a Skills repo means Your private copy of learning-room-template. Mona (GitHub) means Aria (PR validation) and the Student Progression bot.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. This part earns its place because when the facilitators pair you with another participant for Challenge 3 or Challenge 8, you will be added as a collaborator on their Learning Room repo. That gives the learner a foothold: here is how to find the PRs they want you to look at. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Finding PRs to Review. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Finding PRs to Review: Screen reader users (VoiceOver - macOS).

speaker: ALEX
text: First, go to your peer's Learning Room repo (the URL the facilitators sent you, or open it from your GitHub Notifications inbox). Then, click the Pull Requests tab. After that, click the Filters dropdown - "Review requested" - your username. Finally, click any PR title to open it. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Go to your peer's Learning Room repo; 2. Press D - "Repository navigation"; 3. Press K - navigate to "Pull Requests" tab; 4. Filter: Press F, type "review-requested:@me"; 5. Press H repeatedly to navigate PR titles; 6. Press Enter to open a PR. Go to your peer's Learning Room repo; 2. VO+U - Landmarks - "Repository navigation"; 3. Quick Nav K - navigate to "Pull Requests" tab - VO+Space; 4. Filter: Quick Nav F, type "review-requested:@me", press Return; 5. Quick Nav H (or VO+Cmd+H) to navigate PR. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Here is the learner-facing version. Screen reader users (VoiceOver - macOS).

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Conversation tab: Scroll through the discussion. Reviewers are listed in the right sidebar. Files Changed tab: Changed files are in a tree on the left. Click a filename to jump to its diff. Green = added lines, red = removed lines. Line comments appear as inline cards within the diff.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Leaving a Review. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Leaving a Review: screen reader users (VoiceOver - macOS).

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Scroll to the comment box on the Conversation tab; Type your review comment; Click "Review Changes" (top-right of the Files Changed tab, or at the bottom of the PR page); and Select your review type: Comment / Approve / Request changes. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Leaving a Review, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 1 checks: Click "Submit review". That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like On Conversation tab, scroll to comment box; 2. Switch to Focus Mode (NVDA+Space / Insert+Z); 3. Type your review comment; 4. Tab to "Review Changes" button; 5. Select review type:; - "Comment" (just feedback); - "Approve" (good to merge); - "Request changes". Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Responding to Feedback. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on Responding to Feedback. Screen reader users (VoiceOver - macOS). The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Open your PR (Pull Requests tab → click your PR); Read all comments and bot feedback; Click in the comment box to reply; and Push your fixes to the same branch. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Responding to Feedback, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is comment: "Updates pushed, ready for review". The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Open your PR (find in Pull Requests tab); 2. Read all comments and bot feedback; 3. Scroll to comment box; 4. Type your response; 5. Mention reviewers with @ if clarifying; 6. Push your fixes to the same branch; 7. Comment: "Updates pushed, ready for review". Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Tips for PR Sharing. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Tips for PR Sharing has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. To find PRs assigned to you for review, navigate to Pull Requests tab and type review-requested:@me in the filter field; press Enter to apply. On the Files Changed tab, press 3 to jump between file headings in the diff; press Tab to navigate to inline comment buttons. When leaving a review, press Tab from the comment box to reach the "Review Changes" button; the review type selector uses radio buttons navigable with Arrow keys. In the Files Changed tab, additions are highlighted in green and deletions in red; use a high-contrast theme if these colors are hard to distinguish. Inline review comments appear as small text boxes embedded in the diff; at high zoom they may be narrow -- resize the browser window wider if text wraps awkwardly. The "Submit review" button changes appearance based on your selected review type; "Approve" shows a green icon, "Request changes" shows a red icon.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Do not treat "Can I see other students' PRs?" as decoration. Not inside their Learning Room repos -- those are private to each student. That is not trivia. You can see other participants' work in two ways.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. During Challenge 3 ("Join the Conversation") and Challenge 8 ("Culture"), the facilitators pair you with classmates and add you as a collaborator on each other's repos so you can review. During Day 2 (and the Bonus C challenge), everyone contributes to the public accessibility-agents repo, where every PR is visible to everyone.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on "What if I don't agree with my assigned reviewer?". What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, "What if I don't agree with my assigned reviewer?" is still useful because when the facilitators pair you for peer review, the pairing is a starting point, not a mandate. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: You can request additional reviewers manually.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put "Will my PR get lost when everyone is working at once?" into plain language. Your repo is your own; you only see your own PRs. The useful version is: Aria's feedback is on your PR alone, and any peer reviewer is specifically assigned to you. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on "Can I comment on someone else's PR?". What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. When the facilitators pair you for review, yes -- you will be added as a collaborator and can comment, approve, and request changes on their PR. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: On the public accessibility-agents repo, anyone can comment on any open PR.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because mention them directly in a PR comment: "@name, any thoughts on the changes I pushed?" Or ask a facilitator to follow up.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on "Can I work with a friend?". What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: "Can I work with a friend?": The facilitators arrange peer pairings, but if you know someone else in the cohort and you want to review each other's work, ask either Jeff or Michael to add you to each other's repos.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. When pairings happen during a workshop block, typically 15-60 minutes. Put another way, if a reviewer is slow, the facilitators can step in or assign someone else. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on "What if bot feedback is wrong?". What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside "What if bot feedback is wrong?": Aria is intentionally educational, not punitive -- if you disagree with a check, the facilitators can override it. That matters in practice: Aria is not perfect, which is exactly why human review still matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on "Do I need to complete every challenge?". The Learning Room has challenges for all skill levels. This is the part to say slowly: You can pick what interests you, complete at your pace, and continue after the workshop -- your repo stays yours.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Celebration: You're Contributing. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: every PR you open and merge in the Learning Room is a real contribution. The listener should be able to check this: You found something to improve You made a meaningful change You received feedback (technical + human) You incorporated suggestions You merged your work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 4. Next in the series is episode 5, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
